# Abstract


# Introduction

Social vulnerability reflects how community-level social and demographic conditions influence the ability of populations to prepare for, withstand, and recover from hazards. In disaster management and public health, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention / Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) has become an important tool for identifying areas where communities may face disproportionate risk due to factors such as socioeconomic disadvantage, household composition, minority status, housing conditions, and transportation access (Flanagan et al., 2011; CDC/ATSDR, 2024). The index was designed to support emergency planning and resource allocation by identifying communities that may require additional assistance before, during, and after disasters (Flanagan et al., 2011). Recent evaluations of the index have further shown that SVI performs particularly well in preparedness, response, and recovery contexts for moderate and severe hazards, although its predictive ability is more limited in sudden extreme events where physical exposure may dominate outcomes (CDC/ATSDR, 2024).

The SVI is constructed using county-level socioeconomic indicators derived from the U.S. Census Bureau American Community Survey. These indicators are aggregated into four vulnerability themes—Socioeconomic Status, Household Characteristics, Racial and Ethnic Minority Status, and Housing Type and Transportation—and percentile ranks are then calculated to produce the theme scores used in the index (Flanagan et al., 2011; CDC/ATSDR, 2024). Because these percentile scores are derived directly from the underlying indicators, predicting SVI theme rankings using the EP_* indicators can be interpreted as learning the transformation that maps socioeconomic conditions to vulnerability percentiles rather than identifying entirely new predictors of vulnerability. From this perspective, the modeling task focuses on learning and approximating the SVI scoring structure, providing insight into **how different machine learning architectures capture the nonlinear relationships embedded within the index**.

Motivated by this perspective, the present study models SVI within a regression framework that integrates deep learning and spatial graph-based methods to examine how vulnerability patterns vary across geographically connected communities. Counties are represented as nodes within a geographic graph in which neighboring regions are connected through spatial adjacency relationships, allowing the analysis to incorporate spatial neighborhood context when predicting vulnerability scores. By comparing a tabular deep learning baseline **(Multilayer Perceptron (MLP))** with **Graph Convolutional Network (GCN)** models, the study investigates whether explicitly modeling spatial structure improves county-level SVI prediction beyond conventional non-spatial approaches. In doing so, the study explores how neural architectures and spatial relational learning can approximate and potentially enhance the representation of vulnerability patterns embedded in the CDC/ATSDR SVI framework.

## Project Objectives

To guide the scope of the study, the following objectives are defined to progressively move from tabular deep learning models to spatial graph-based learning methods. Because the Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) percentile scores are derived from the same underlying socioeconomic indicators used as predictors in this study, the modeling task is interpreted as **learning and approximating the transformation that maps vulnerability indicators to SVI theme percentiles**. The objectives therefore focus on evaluating how different machine learning architectures capture this mapping and whether incorporating spatial structure improves the representation of vulnerability patterns across geographic regions.

1) Conduct **exploratory data analysis (EDA)** to examine the distribution, scale, and relationships among the Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) indicators, including feature distributions, transformations, and spatial patterns across counties in order to better understand the statistical and geographic structure of the dataset prior to model development.

2) Establish a deep learning baseline for tabular prediction by training a **Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)** to model nonlinear relationships between the EP_* socioeconomic indicators and the SVI theme percentile scores, providing a benchmark for how well neural networks can approximate the SVI scoring transformation using tabular inputs alone.

3) Incorporate spatial structure into prediction by representing **counties as nodes in a geographic graph** constructed from spatial adjacency relationships, enabling neighboring communities to be modeled as relationally connected rather than independent observations.

4) Assess the impact of graph convolutional learning by implementing a **Graph Convolutional Network (GCN)** to determine whether localized neighborhood aggregation improves predictive performance over both tabular and general graph neural models.

5) Compare tabular and graph-based deep learning approaches within a **unified regression framework** to evaluate how model architecture and spatial information influence the ability to approximate county-level SVI percentile scores.

6) Investigate the role of spatial context in social vulnerability modeling by examining whether geographic connectivity between counties provides meaningful predictive signal beyond what is captured by the underlying socioeconomic and demographic indicators alone.

## Related Work

Foundational work on social vulnerability established that disaster impacts are shaped not only by physical exposure to hazards, but also by underlying social and economic conditions that influence a community’s capacity to prepare for, respond to, and recover from disasters. Flanagan et al. (2011) developed the **CDC/ATSDR Social Vulnerability Index (SVI)** as a county-level composite measure intended to support emergency management planning and resource allocation. Since its introduction, SVI has become one of the most widely used operational tools for identifying socially vulnerable communities in the United States. More recently, a CDC/ATSDR validation assessment found that SVI performs favorably overall, particularly for response and recovery contexts associated with moderate and severe hazards. However, the assessment also noted weaker applicability for predicting mortality in sudden, extreme events where physical damage and exposure may dominate outcomes (CDC/ATSDR, 2024).

A second line of research has examined how different social vulnerability indices represent and operationalize the concept of vulnerability. Tarling (2017) compared the CDC SVI and the Social Vulnerability Index (SoVI) and found that methodological choices in index construction can meaningfully influence results. In the case study examined, CDC SVI tended to capture vulnerability primarily through socioeconomic conditions, whereas SoVI appeared more sensitive to demographic factors such as age. These findings highlight that vulnerability estimates depend not only on underlying social conditions, but also on **how variables are selected, aggregated, and represented within the index**.

More recently, machine learning approaches have begun to appear in the social vulnerability literature, though the body of work remains relatively limited and heterogeneous. Zhao et al. (2024) explored the use of machine learning and expanded data sources for constructing social vulnerability indices across multiple countries, demonstrating that nonlinear models and richer datasets can reveal important vulnerability drivers such as wealth disparities. Similarly, Kalaycioglu et al. applied several supervised learning algorithms to predict household-level social vulnerability in Istanbul and found that artificial neural networks performed best among the evaluated models, suggesting that machine learning methods may better capture complex interactions among socioeconomic variables. Yokoyama and Takefuji further examined machine-learning models built using CDC SVI variables and emphasized that model interpretability should be evaluated relative to classical statistical associations rather than inferred solely from predictive performance. In a related public health application, Yedinak et al. incorporated CDC SVI indicators alongside other county-level predictors in a machine learning framework for neighborhood-level vulnerability assessment, illustrating how vulnerability-related measures can support applied risk stratification.

Despite these advances, an important gap remains in the literature. Existing machine learning studies on social vulnerability typically rely on tabular modeling approaches and do not explicitly incorporate spatial relationships between geographic units. Most prior work either predicts a pre-constructed vulnerability score, evaluates feature importance within machine learning models, or uses SVI variables as inputs to related downstream prediction tasks. To the best of current knowledge, there is **limited work applying graph neural networks (GNNs) to the CDC/ATSDR SVI county-level dataset** in order to explicitly model spatial relationships among counties. This gap motivates the present study, which extends existing tabular machine learning approaches by introducing a spatially relational learning framework that represents counties as nodes within a geographic graph. By incorporating adjacency relationships between neighboring counties, graph-based models provide a natural mechanism for capturing spatial dependence that may influence patterns of social vulnerability across regions.

# Data Summary

The county-level 2022 Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) dataset is a relative vulnerability dataset designed to identify counties that may be more vulnerable before, during, and after hazardous events. The index was developed by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and the Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry to support disaster preparedness, response planning, and public health resource allocation (Flanagan et al., 2011; CDC/ATSDR, 2024). The dataset integrates socioeconomic indicators derived primarily from the U.S. Census Bureau American Community Survey and converts them into percentile-based vulnerability rankings across U.S. counties.

In the official documentation, SVI variables are organized through a structured pipeline of derived fields. These include raw estimate fields (E_), margin-of-error fields (M_), derived percentage fields (EP_), percentage margin-of-error fields (MP_), percentile-ranked indicator fields (EPL_), theme sums (SPL_), theme percentile rankings (RPL_THEME1 through RPL_THEME4), and the overall percentile ranking (RPL_THEMES). Higher percentile values correspond to **greater relative social vulnerability compared with other counties**.

Because the percentile rankings are computed directly from the underlying EP_* indicators, the modeling task in this study focuses on **learning the transformation that maps socioeconomic indicators to SVI theme percentile scores rather than discovering entirely new vulnerability predictors**. The machine learning models therefore approximate the functional relationship embedded within the official SVI scoring framework while evaluating how different model architectures capture these relationships.

## Data Sources

1. US County 2022 SVI dataset: https://www.atsdr.cdc.gov/place-health/php/svi/svi-data-documentation-download.html
   
2. US County 2022 SVI dataset documentation: https://svi.cdc.gov/map25/data/docs/SVI2022Documentation_ZCTA.pdf 

3. US County 2022 Tiger Line: https://catalog.data.gov/dataset/tiger-line-shapefile-2022-nation-u-s-county-and-equivalent-entities/resource/1eb8657f-0109-4712-a714-32a569edc1ad?

The TIGER/Line geographic data provide county boundary geometries used to construct spatial adjacency relationships for the graph-based models developed later in the study.

## Geographic and Base Reference Fields

At the county level, the dataset includes geographic identifiers and reference attributes used for data integration, filtering, and mapping. Key fields include:

- ST: state-level FIPS code

- STATE: state name

- ST_ABBR: state abbreviation

- STCNTY: county-level FIPS code

- COUNTY: county name

- FIPS: geographic identification field used by SVI (**Primary geographic identifier used for dataset joins**)

- LOCATION: text description of geography

- AREA_SQMI: area in square miles

The dataset also includes population and housing reference counts used in percentage calculations:

- E_TOTPOP / M_TOTPOP: total population estimate and MOE

- E_HU / M_HU: housing units estimate and MOE

- E_HH / M_HH: households estimate and MOE

These counts provide the denominators used when computing the EP_* percentage indicators.

## Core SVI Feature Set: 16 Social Vulnerability Variables

The primary predictive feature set consists of 16 socioeconomic indicators, organized into four themes within the official SVI framework. These indicators are expressed as percentages (EP_* variables) derived from underlying American Community Survey estimates.

  1) Theme 1: Socioeconomic Status

  This theme captures economic hardship and limited access to resources. Its core variables are:

  - E_POV150 / EP_POV150: persons below 150% poverty; percentage of persons below 150% poverty

  - E_UNEMP / EP_UNEMP: civilian unemployed age 16+; unemployment rate

  - E_HBURD / EP_HBURD: housing cost-burdened occupied housing units with annual income less than $75,000, defined as spending more than 30% of income on housing costs; corresponding percentage

  - E_NOHSDP / EP_NOHSDP: persons age 25+ with no high school diploma; corresponding percentage

  - E_UNINSUR / EP_UNINSUR: uninsured civilian noninstitutionalized population; corresponding percentage
  
  2) Theme 2: Household Characteristics

    This theme captures age-related dependency, disability, family structure, and language vulnerability. Its core variables are:

    - E_AGE65 / EP_AGE65: persons aged 65 and older; corresponding percentage

    - E_AGE17 / EP_AGE17: persons aged 17 and younger; corresponding percentage

    - E_DISABL / EP_DISABL: civilian noninstitutionalized population with a disability; corresponding percentage

    - E_SNGPNT / EP_SNGPNT: single-parent households with children under 18; corresponding percentage

    - E_LIMENG / EP_LIMENG: persons age 5+ who speak English “less than well”; corresponding percentage

  3) Theme 3: Racial & Ethnic Minority Status

    This theme is represented by a single aggregated minority-status variable:

    - E_MINRTY / EP_MINRTY: minority population estimate and percentage, defined in the documentation as persons who are Hispanic or Latino of any race, or non-Hispanic persons who are Black or African American, American Indian and Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian and Other Pacific Islander, two or more races, or some other race

  4) Theme 4: Housing Type and Transportation

    This theme captures housing structure, crowding, and transportation access. Its core variables are:

    - E_MUNIT / EP_MUNIT: housing in structures with 10 or more units; corresponding percentage

    - E_MOBILE / EP_MOBILE: mobile homes; corresponding percentage

    - E_CROWD / EP_CROWD: occupied housing units with more people than rooms; corresponding percentage

    - E_NOVEH / EP_NOVEH: households with no vehicle available; corresponding percentage

    - E_GROUPQ / EP_GROUPQ: persons in group quarters; corresponding percentage

## Margin-of-Error Fields

For each estimate and many derived percentages, the dataset includes corresponding margin-of-error fields derived from American Community Survey sampling variability. Fields beginning with M_ represent margins of error for estimates, while fields beginning with MP_ represent margins of error for percentages.

Examples include M_POV150, M_UNEMP, M_AGE65, M_MINRTY, M_GROUPQ, and the percentage MOE counterparts such as MP_POV150, MP_LIMENG, and MP_GROUPQ.

Although these values provide important information about statistical uncertainty, they are not directly used as predictors in the machine learning models developed in this study.

## Ranked and Summary Output Fields

After computing the EP_* percentage indicators, the SVI framework converts each indicator into percentile rankings (EPL_*). These percentiles are then aggregated within themes to produce theme summary scores (SPL_THEME1–SPL_THEME4). The percentile rankings of these theme summaries form the primary outcome variables:

1) RPL_THEME1: Socioeconomic Status

2) RPL_THEME2: Household Characteristics

3) RPL_THEME3: Racial & Ethnic Minority Status

4) RPL_THEME4: Housing Type & Transportation

The overall summary score is SPL_THEMES, and the overall county vulnerability percentile is RPL_THEMES. In practice, RPL_THEMES is the main overall SVI outcome variable. 

This study focuses on the **theme-level percentile rankings (RPL_THEME1–RPL_THEME4)** and the overall SVI percentile ranking (RPL_THEMES). The feature matrix primarily uses the **percentage-based vulnerability variables (EP_*)**, which represent normalized forms of the underlying estimates.

# Model Design & Training Procedure

This section provides a brief overview of the modeling approach and training pipeline used in this study. The following sections present the motivation for each model, the mathematical formulations, and the experimental and analytical results in greater detail.

To evaluate whether spatial structure improves prediction of social vulnerability, three model types were implemented within a unified regression framework: a multilayer perceptron (MLP) baseline and a graph convolutional network (GCN). Each model predicts the continuous Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) score at the county level using socioeconomic, demographic, and housing-related features derived from the CDC/ATSDR dataset.

The primary predictor variables used in this study are the **16 EP_* variables** from the CDC/ATSDR SVI 2022 dataset. These variables represent estimated percentages of populations experiencing specific vulnerability-related conditions, such as poverty, unemployment, limited English proficiency, minority status, disability, housing crowding, and lack of vehicle access. Using the EP_* variables as the main features provides several advantages: 

   1) These measures directly capture the underlying social and demographic characteristics that contribute to vulnerability rather than the aggregated composite index itself. 
   
   2) Percentage-based variables provide normalized representations of population characteristics, allowing counties of different sizes to be compared on a consistent scale.
   
   3) Using the EP_* variables aligns with the conceptual design of the SVI, where the overall index is derived from these component indicators, making them appropriate explanatory variables for modeling county-level variation in vulnerability.

The MLP baseline serves as a tabular deep learning model that **learns nonlinear relationships among county-level features while treating each county as an independent observation**. In contrast, the graph-based models incorporate spatial structure by representing counties as nodes in a geographic graph constructed using spatial adjacency relationships. Edges in this graph connect neighboring counties based on Queen contiguity, allowing information to propagate between geographically adjacent regions during training.

The model follows the Graph Neural Network (GNN) framework, where node representations are updated through message passing that aggregates feature information from neighboring counties. In this study, the message-passing mechanism is implemented using a **Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) architecture**. The GCN layer performs graph convolution operations that aggregate normalized neighborhood features, enabling localized spatial smoothing and structured feature propagation across the county network.

All models are trained as **node-level regression models** to predict continuous SVI-theme values. Prior to training, feature variables are transformed and standardized based on insights from exploratory data analysis to address skewness and scale differences across variables. For graph-based models, the processed features serve as node attributes, while the spatial adjacency matrix defines the graph structure used during message passing.

Training is conducted using supervised learning with a **regression loss function**, and models are optimized using **stochastic gradient–based methods**. The dataset is divided into training, validation, and test subsets. The training set is used to fit model parameters, while the validation set is used for hyperparameter tuning, early stopping, and model selection. After model configuration is finalized, performance is evaluated on the held-out test set to obtain an unbiased estimate of predictive accuracy and generalization. This evaluation framework enables consistent comparison between the tabular MLP baseline and the spatial graph-based models in order to determine whether explicitly modeling geographic relationships improves county-level SVI prediction.
